draft.introduction: Plutarch’s Life of Alexander (c. 100 CE) is the most influential ancient biography of Alexander the Great. Plutarch focuses on character rather than military strategy, opening with the famous declaration that he writes lives, not histories.

translation.text: In this book I write the life of Alexander the king and of Caesar who overthrew Pompey. The multitude of the deeds to be treated is so great that I shall make no other preface than to beg my readers not to complain if I do not report all of the famous actions in full detail, but abbreviate most of them. For it is not histories that I am writing, but lives; and in the most illustrious deeds there is not always a manifestation of virtue or vice. Often a small thing, a word or a jest, reveals character more than battles where thousands die.
translation.original_text: τὸν Ἀλεξάνδρου τοῦ βασιλέως βίον καὶ τὸν Καίσαρος

translation.text: Alexander came to the tomb of Achilles, and after anointing the pillar with oil and running naked with his companions, as the custom is, he crowned it with garlands, declaring Achilles fortunate to have found Homer as the herald of his fame. And indeed, Alexander had good reason to envy Achilles this advantage, for his own great deeds had no worthy recorder.
translation.original_text: Ἀλέξανδρος δὲ πρὸς τὸν Ἀχιλλέως τάφον ἐλθών

translator_notes.note: Plutarch’s distinction between “lives” and “histories” (οὔτε γὰρ ἱστορίας γράφομεν ἀλλὰ βίους) is a foundational statement of biographical method. He is interested in the soul’s character, revealed through small incidents, not the comprehensive record of events. This approach influenced all subsequent Western biography.
